title: Investor-Paid Credit Ratings and Managerial Information Disclosure
abstract: Unlike issuer-paid credit rating agencies (CRAs), investor-paid CRAs are compensated by investors for providing rating services. Exploiting the staggered timing of rating initiation by an investor-paid rating agency (the Egan Jones Ratings (EJR)), I document that the coverage by EJR increases rated firm managers’ voluntary disclosure of negative news. Consistent with EJR’s rating coverage deterring managerial bad news hoarding by informing investors of downside risks, I find that the effect of EJR coverage is more pronounced when issuer-paid CRAs tend to assign inflated ratings and when rated firms’ managers have a stronger incentive to conceal bad news. I also document that firms unwind upward earnings management after being covered by EJR. In contrast, coverage by an issuer-paid CRA (Standard & Poor’s) is not associated with changes in managerial information disclosure. I conclude that investor-paid CRAs function as a type of effective information intermediary to discipline firm managers and improve corporate transparency.

author.Wei Li: School of Accountancy, Shanghai University of Finance and Economics, Shanghai 200437, China

article.1. Institutional Background and Hypothes: 1.1. Institutional Background Established in 1995, the Egan Jones Ratings Corporation is a rating company that derives its revenue from equity and debt investors (Badoer et al. 2019,Bhattacharya et al. 2019). Since its foundation, EJR has relied on its unique compensation structure to distinguish itself from other major issuer-paid CRAs. EJR has gradually established a reputation of providing timely and accurate ratings that serve investors’ demand for high-quality information. In what follows, I briefly outline the key differences between the rating process of EJR and S&P and link these differences to the efficiency of their information production and monitoring. EJR differs from issuer-paid CRAs in almost every stage of the rating process. First, issuer-paid CRAs generally initiate rating coverage at the request of debt issuers, who are required by regulation to obtain credit ratings for their public bond issuance. In contrast, EJR generally initiates rating coverage at the request of investors, specifically, institutional investors.9Subscribing investors have full access to all of EJR’s current and historical ratings as well as rating updates for all companies covered by EJR. Second, EJR differs from traditional issuer-paid CRAs in the rating production and dissemination process. In terms of rating production, EJR and S&P both rely on rating models that synthesize quantitative and qualitative factors. Qualitative factors include management actions, earnings quality, and major corporate events such as mergers and acquisitions. An important difference in the rating process is that issuer-paid rating analysts may have better access to material nonpublic information from issuers than do investor-paid credit analysts (Bonsall et al. 2017). Despite this advantage in the form of better information access, issuer-paid ratings still generally underperform investor-paid ratings regarding rating quality. To further illustrate how the eventual rating products differ across the two types of CRA, I retrieve samples of credit rating reports for EJR and S&P.10A comparison of the two sample reports reveals an important difference in their rating focus: EJR’s rating reports appear to be more investor-oriented than S&P’s report. As highlighted by the report excerpts inFigure A.1, EJR’s report contains valuation analyses for both debt investors and equity investors in the form of default recovery rates for the former and stock price valuation and buyout feasibility analyses for the latter.11In contrast, S&P’s rating reports are more debt-holder oriented because they focus primarily on several key measures, including debt-to-profitability and debt-to–cash flow ratio, which reflect firms’ current ability to pay back their debt obligations. Thus, the comparison suggests that EJR’s rating reports seem to perform better at synthesizing and interpreting information for firm valuation and can be more easily translated into investment decisions. After ratings are produced, EJR adopts a unique rating dissemination strategy. It first discloses the rating reports to investors who pay for its rating services and, at a later date, makes only the rating (not the full report) publicly available to all investors at no cost on its company website and the Bloomberg terminal.12The preferential disclosure of ratings to subscribing investors gives them a first mover advantage and avoids free riding by nonsubscribing investors. The subsequent disclosure to all investors helps EJR enhance its market impact by attracting investors’ attention and media coverage. Finally, regarding postissuance monitoring, both types of CRA claim that they continue to monitor issuers after bond issuance. For example, EJR states that, in addition to regularly monitoring issuers’ fundamentals, it screens rated firms on a monthly basis using a quantitative model. The continuous monitoring of debt issuers enables EJR to flag issuers with potential changes in fundamentals, guiding investors’ attention and research efforts. Although issuer-paid CRAs such as S&P claim that they continue to monitor the credit risk of issuers, previous research shows that issuer-paid CRAs exert less effort on monitoring following issuers’ bond issuance, and their ratings become less informative about credit risk (e.g.,Bonsall et al. 2015). Issuer-paid CRAs’ lax postissuance monitoring likely is due to the lack of a long-term incentive in their compensation structure. Most of their revenue from an issuer is generated at the debt issuance date, whereas the fees from ongoing screening are much lower (Rosner 2009). In contrast, EJR derives revenue from its customers’ annual subscription fees. Thus, we should expect EJR to maintain its rating efforts and rating quality by providing closer postissuance surveillance than S&P.13 1.2. Related Literature and Hypothesis The literature on investor-paid CRAs largely focuses on the informativeness of investor-paid ratings relative to issuer-paid ratings.Beaver et al. (2006)document that EJR’s ratings lead Moody’s ratings and are accompanied by greater stock price movements and attribute this finding to the significant role of Moody’s ratings in regulation and contracting.Bruno et al. (2016)confirm the findings ofBeaver et al. (2006)using the sample period following EJR’s certification as a nationally recognized statistical rating organization (NRSRO) in 2007 but conclude that it is the difference in their compensation structures, not the regulatory and contracting role, that drives the differences in rating properties between EJR and Moody’s.Jiang et al. (2012)show that S&P’s ratings become more inflated following its switch in 1974 from an investor-pay to an issuer-pay model.14Xia (2014)shows that S&P’s rating quality improves after EJR starts to cover the same firm because EJR’s entry raises S&P’s reputational concerns.Cornaggia and Cornaggia (2013)andBhattacharya et al. (2019)estimate the effect of rating quality on investors’ wealth and find that trading on investor-paid ratings delivers better portfolio performance. The collective evidence suggests that, in the absence of incentives to cater to issuers’ desire for favorable ratings, investor-paid CRAs tend to provide more informative ratings than issuer-paid CRAs. Despite a growing literature on the rating properties of investor-paid CRAs, a fundamental question remains unanswered: can investor-paid CRAs have real impacts on rated firms’ transparency? Although the comparison between investor- and issuer-paid CRAs provide important insights about their rating quality, the relative timeliness and accuracy of the former over the latter are not sufficient to conclude that investor-paid CRAs bring new information to investors, especially in transparent markets in which security prices already aggregate diverse pieces of information from investors (Badoer and Demiroglu 2019). It is, thus, important to directly evaluate whether investor-paid CRAs’ relative advantage over issuer-paid CRAs improves the information environment at the firm level. This paper takes a step in this direction by studying whether the rating coverage of an investor-paid CRA (i.e., EJR) can discipline managerial information disclosure. I develop my hypothesis based on theories of corporate voluntary disclosure and empirical findings on the effectiveness of EJR’s credit ratings to inform investors. Theories on management voluntary disclosure predict that, when investors acquire new information, they update their interpretations of corporate disclosure or nondisclosure, which, in turn, shape managers’ disclosure motives. The models ofJung and Kwon (1988),Dye and Sridhar (1995), andDye (1998)predict that, when the probability of investors becoming informed increases (through their information acquisition or other firms’ disclosure), firms withhold less information because investors have a better understanding about the cause of nondisclosure, that is, whether nondisclosure is due to managers not receiving information or withholding the received information. The reduction in uncertainty about the existence of information pressures firm managers to provide more disclosure. Importantly, these models predict that the disclosure effect works mainly for negative news because, if investors are uninformed about managers’ information endowment, managers tend to withhold bad news but disclose good news earlier (e.g.,Kothari et al. 2009).15 Empirical evidence suggests that EJR’s credit ratings convey valuable information to investors. Specifically,Beaver et al. (2006)finds that EJR’s ratings are timelier and more accurate than S&P’s ratings, andBruno et al. (2016)document that the superior timeliness and accuracy of EJR’s ratings persist after its NRSRO designation.Cornaggia and Cornaggia (2013)show that investors who follow EJR’s ratings earn a higher return than other nonfollowers on the debt market. Perhaps the most compelling evidence that EJR’s ratings have information value is that a group of institutional equity investors consistently purchase and trade on EJR’s ratings (Bhattacharya et al. 2019). Furthermore, by summarizing and curating information from various sources, ratings serve as easy-to-process trading signals. Investors facing information processing constraints naturally pay more attention to such signals and the underlying firms (Hirshleifer and Teoh 2003). Consequently, by informing investors about the existence of news and guiding their attention to firms that warrant investigation efforts, EJR can play a similar disciplining role in managerial disclosure as other market participants, including short-sellers and media (e.g.,Bushee et al. 2010,Li and Zhang 2015). These theoretical and empirical discussions lead to the hypothesis that the coverage of investor-paid ratings pushes managers to disclose more information—primarily negative information—to the public.
article.2. Data and Sample Construction: 2.1. Credit Rating Data I choose EJR as the representative investor-paid CRA among several other investor-paid CRAs.16This choice is motivated by the following two reasons. First, EJR has become a leading investor-paid CRA in the corporate rating industry, rating more than 1,000 listed firms in the United States. In addition, EJR’s ratings are publicly available. It grants researchers access to its historical ratings for all firms upon request. Thus, EJR’s rating data are also used in other studies (e.g.,Beaver et al. 2006,Bhattacharya et al. 2019). Following prior studies (e.g.,Xia 2014) viewing issuer-paid CRAs as a homogeneous group, I use S&P as the representative issuer-paid rating agency. EJR has provided credit ratings at the request of investors since 1995. However, its ratings are not available before July 1999. I exclude firms that receive EJR coverage before this date (because the exact rating initiation dates for these firms are unavailable) as well as firms that obtain an initial rating but are not followed by EJR ever since. The EJR credit rating data set is manually matched by historical company names with Compustat. I successfully match 994 firms covered by EJR to Compustat firms. To compare the effect of EJR’s coverage with that of S&P’s coverage, I also obtain S&P’s long-term issuer credit ratings from Compustat. I obtain management forecast data from the Institutional Brokers’ Estimate System (IBES) database, financial statement data from Compustat, and common stock return data from the Center for Research in Security Prices. Institutional ownership and trading data are from the Thomson Reuters ownership database compiled from 13f filings. Following earlier studies (e.g.,Rickmann 2022), I exclude financial firms (Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) code 6000–6999) and firm–year observations with missing control variables. These restrictions led to 616 Compustat firms covered by EJR during 1999–2016. 2.2. Variables To identify when EJR starts to cover a firm at the request of investors, I use the first EJR credit rating date for a firm as the coverage initiation date. I then define a dummy variable,EJR Cover, which equals one in a year if a firm has been rated by EJR until that year and zero otherwise. I construct a measure of management disclosure of bad news based on management earnings guidance from the IBES Guidance database. Following previous studies (e.g.,Bao et al. 2019), I include range and point estimates for both quarterly and annual earnings guidance. For point (range) estimates, the maximum or minimum (midpoint) is taken as the guided value, which is then compared with the most recent analyst earnings forecast consensus. Management forecasts that fall short of (beat) the contemporaneous analyst consensus are classified as bad (good) news disclosure. To isolate the effect of EJR coverage on management disclosure, I include in the regression a standard set of control variables used in previous studies (Li and Yang 2016,Bao et al. 2019). Specifically, I include the natural logarithm of the market capitalization (Ln(Market Cap)) to account for the effect of size on EJR’s coverage (e.g.,Xia 2014), the market value of equity plus book value of debt scaled by the book value of total assets (MTB) for growth opportunities, the natural log of the number of analysts following (Ln(Analysts)) for the corporate information environment, and institutional ownership (IO) for the potential demand for EJR’s ratings.17Because investors’ concern for firms’ credit quality and risk profile may drive their demand for EJR’s ratings (Bonsall et al. 2017), I add leverage ratio, Altman’sZ-score, and the volatility of return on equity and stock return in the regression. I include a set of firm performance measures (i.e., return on equity, stock market return, and a loss indicator) that are directly related to the likelihood of bad news. I also control for S&P’s coverage by including a dummy variable,S&P’s Cover, which equals one for firm–years with S&P’s credit ratings and zero otherwise. Corporate financing activities (Debt IssuanceandEquity Issuance) are added because they may drive investors’ demand for credit rating and firms’ disclosure at the same time. Finally, I include the following control variables used in previous studies focusing on corporate transparency and disclosure:Special itemis a special item on the income statement scaled by total assets (e.g.,Glaeser 2018),R&Dis research and development expenses scaled by total assets with missing values set to zero, andD(Missing R&D)indicates firm–years with missing R&D expenses. 2.3. Matched Control Firms Both EJR and S&P are profit-driven corporations; hence, their decisions to rate specific firms are by no means exogenous. If investors’ demand for EJR’s rating services is correlated with other corporate information events, my estimation of EJR’s impact on management disclosure is subject to selection bias. To mitigate this concern, I match each EJR-rated firm to nonrated control firms and use both rated and control firms in the matched sample throughout the analyses. To construct the matched sample, I model investors’ demand for EJR’s rating coverage as a function of firm characteristics. Specifically, I estimate the following probit model with the EJR-rated firms and all other firms from the universe of Compustat between 1999 and 2016 that have nonmissing values for variables used in the regression: The marginal effects of the probit model regressions are reported inTable 1. For comparison, I use the model to forecast the coverage initiations of both EJR and S&P. The forecasting results for EJR and S&P differ in some noteworthy dimensions. First, the pseudoR2of 0.377 in column (1) is considerably higher than the pseudoR2of 0.228 in column (2), suggesting that the model performs much better at predicting the coverage decisions of EJR than those of S&P.19Second, investors are 2.4 percentage points (or 23.1% compared with the unconditional probability of EJR coverage initiation in the sample) more likely to request EJR’s ratings despite the availability of S&P’s ratings, suggesting that investors view EJR’s ratings as providing benefits incremental to S&P’s ratings. In contrast, EJR’s coverage has a much lower dependence on S&P’s coverage status. Finally, the differences in the estimated coefficients for the explanatory variables between columns (1) and (2) suggest differences in EJR’s and S&P’s rating motives. For example, investor demand for EJR’s rating service is more sensitive to firm sales increases. Older firms and firms with lower return volatility are more (less) likely to receive EJR’s (S&P’s) coverage. S&P’s rating coverage is more responsive to firms’ financing activities, consistent with the certification role of issuer-paid ratings (Bongaerts et al. 2012). Table 1.Determinants of Rating Coverage Table 1.Determinants of Rating Coverage Notes.Panel A of this table reports the marginal effects of probit regressions on the determinants of EJR and S&P coverage initiations. The sample consists of Compustat firms from 1999 to 2016 with nonmissing values for variables used in the model. In the first (second) column, the dependent variable is a dummy variable indicating the year when EJR (S&P) starts to rate a firm; firm–years are dropped from the sample once a firm is covered by EJR or S&P. Both regressions control for year and industry fixed effects. Standard errors in parentheses are clustered at the firm level. Panel B reports the comparison of firm characteristics between EJR-rated and matched control firms. For each EJR-rated firm, I find up to three matched firms with the closest predicted likelihood of EJR rating coverage based on the regression in column (1) of panel A. The differences in predicted likelihood between event firms and their control firms are required to be less than 1%. Columns (1) and (2) report the mean of firm characteristics for the event firms and their matched controls. Columns (3) and (4) reports the differences in mean and thep-values of tests for mean differences. Variable definitions are provided inTable A.1. *, **, and *** denote significance level at 10%, 5%, and 1%, respectively. I compute the predicted probability (p) of EJR’s rating coverage for each firm–year based on the regression results in column (1). For each EJR-rated firm in the year prior to EJR’s coverage initiation, I find up to three control firms with the closest propensity scoreΦ−1⁡(𝑝)Φ−1⁡(p)and without EJR coverage in the 10-year window bracketing the matching year. To ensure matching quality, I require the difference in propensity score between each EJR-rated firm and its matched control firm to be less than 1%. The matching procedure identifies 477 control firms for 313 EJR-rated firms, generating a total of 6,144 firm–years between 1999 and 2016. Panel B ofTable 1provides the comparison of firm characteristics between EJR-rated firms (EJR firms) and their control firms (non-EJR firms) in the matching year. Columns (1) and (2) list the average values for EJR-rated firms and their matched control firms, respectively. Columns (3) and (4) list the difference in mean value between the two subsamples and thep-value of mean comparison tests, respectively. First, the two groups of firms do not differ from each other in terms of bad news forecast frequency before EJR’s rating coverage initiations. Second, the comparison suggests that EJR firms are not statistically different from their matched control firms across the long list of characteristics included in the probit regression as all of thep-values are not significant at the conventional level. Finally, both groups of firms have similar probabilities of EJR coverage initiation. Thus, the comparison suggests that the matching procedure is effective in ensuring covariates similarity between EJR-rated and control firms. 2.4. Summary Statistics Table 2presents summary statistics for variables used in the analyses of management forecasts based on the matched sample. Firms in the sample issue an average of 0.65 bad news forecasts and 0.42 good news forecasts. When the market reaction is used to classify management forecasts, I observe an annual average of 0.87 and 0.86 guidance for bad and good news forecasts, respectively. The average number of management forecasts (7.05) is much higher because only earnings forecasts or unbundled forecasts are used to categorize as good or bad news.20Of the sample firm–years, 16.6% have EJR’s rating coverage. Table 2.Summary Statistics Table 2.Summary Statistics Notes.This table provides summary statistics for the variables used in regressions. The sample consists of the stacked matched sample with both EJR-rated firms and their control firms. Financial firms and firms with missing variables used in regressionEquation (2)are all excluded. All values are in 2010 dollars. The definitions of variables are provided inTable A.1. All continuous variables are winsorized at the 1st and 99th percentiles.
article.3. Empirical Results: 3.1. The Baseline Model EJR’s staggered first time rating initiations facilitate a DiD research design that tracks changes in management forecasts before and after the initiations as opposed to the before–after differences for matched control firms. However, recent advances in econometric methods suggest that DiD estimates in a simple TWFE regression are subject to bias when treatment effects are time-varying, and earlier treated units serve as controls for later treated units (e.g.,Barrios 2021,Baker et al. 2022). To alleviate any potential bias, I create a stacked sample of EJR-rated firms and their matched control firms according to the timing of EJR’s rating initiation. Specifically, each year, firms that receive EJR’s rating initiation and their respective matched control firms are regarded as a cohort (or event). I then estimate the TWFE DiD regression with cohort-specific firm and year fixed effects.21The baseline regression takes the following form: Table 3presents the baseline results. In column (1), the regression model includes only theEJR Coverindicator. The estimation returns a coefficient of 0.103 forEJR Coverthat is significant at the 1% level. In the preferred specification in column (2) with all controls included, the coefficient estimate forEJR Coveris 0.100, which remains significant at the conventional level, suggesting that firm characteristics are not confounding the result. The economic magnitude indicates that, within EJR-rated firms, the frequency of bad news earnings guidance increases by 10.5% (i.e.,𝑒0.100−1e0.100−1) when they start to be rated by EJR.22In column (3), to address the concern that industry-specific time-varying factors (e.g., technological advancements or regulatory actions) are driving the relationship between investors’ requests forEJR Coverand bad news forecasts, I replaceYear–cohortwithIndustry–year–cohortfixed effects. The estimate suggests that industry shocks have little effect on the coefficient of interest. Table 3.EJR’s Coverage and Management Guidance Table 3.EJR’s Coverage and Management Guidance Notes.This table reports the baseline regression results regarding how EJR coverage affects rated firms’ management forecasts. The sample consists of firms that are rated by EJR and their matched control firms in the 10-year window around EJR’s coverage. Financial firms are excluded. The dependent variable is the natural log of one plus the frequency of bad news management earnings forecasts in columns (1)–(3), and the natural log of one plus the frequency of good news management earnings forecasts in columns (4)–(6). The independent variableEJR Coveris a dummy variable that equals one if a firm is rated by EJR during the year and zero otherwise.S&P Coveris an indicator that equals one if S&P rates a firm in a year. The definitions of other control variables are provided inTable A.1. The fixed effects included in each regression are denoted at the bottom of the table. Standard errors in parentheses are clustered at the firm level. *, **, and *** denote significance level at 10%, 5%, and 1%, respectively. From columns (4)–(6), I focus on management good news forecasts as the outcome variable. Across the three specifications, the estimated relationship betweenEJR Coverand good news forecasts is indistinguishable from zero. This null result for good news forecasts lends additional credence to the disciplining effect of EJR’s rating coverage on management forecasts because, if contemporaneous information events are driving the relationship between EJR’s rating coverage and management forecasts, we should observe similar effects for both good and bad news forecasts.23Turning to theS&P Coverindicator, the estimate suggests no material relationship between the rating coverage of S&P and management news forecasts. Thus, the results provide initial support for the hypothesis that EJR’s rating coverage disciplines managers to disclose more bad news in the form of management earnings guidance. 3.2. Tests of Endogeneity Whereas the baseline finding indicates a strong positive relationship between EJR coverage and the frequency of bad news forecasts, the causal relationship between the two remains hypothetical. In particular, apart from the selection issues discussed in Section 3.3, the documented results inTable 3could be subject to two types of endogeneity concerns, namely, reverse causality and omitted variable bias. The relationship between EJR coverage and management forecasts could be bidirectional if investors’ expectations about the likelihood of bad news prompt them to seek EJR’s rating service. Furthermore, the estimated coefficient of EJR coverage could be spurious if some omitted variables drive both EJR coverage and management forecasts. I address these concerns in the following analyses. 3.2.1. Reverse Causality Concern. Because of the uncertainty over the existence of bad news, it can be controversial to attribute increases in managers’ voluntary earnings guidance to their commitment to timely bad news disclosure. Thus, a positive relationship between EJR coverage and management bad news guidance may be driven by increases in the frequency of bad news arrival. In what follows, I design tests to assess the validity of this reverse causality concern. I begin by examining the parallel trend assumption underlyingEquation (2)to alleviate the reverse causality concern. Specifically, I check the dynamics of the effect of EJR coverage over the periods before and after EJR’s coverage initiation. If the arrival of bad news is confounding the results, we expect an increase in bad news forecasts prior to EJR’s coverage. To test this possibility, I replace theEJR Coverdummy inEquation (2)with six year dummies:𝐸𝐽𝑅𝐶𝑜𝑣𝑒𝑟−3,𝐸𝐽𝑅𝐶𝑜𝑣𝑒𝑟−2,𝐸𝐽𝑅𝐶𝑜𝑣𝑒𝑟−1,𝐸𝐽𝑅𝐶𝑜𝑣𝑒𝑟0,𝐸𝐽𝑅𝐶𝑜𝑣𝑒𝑟1EJRCover−3,EJRCover−2,EJRCover−1,EJRCover0,EJRCover1, and𝐸𝐽𝑅𝐶𝑜𝑣𝑒𝑟2+EJRCover2+. The first five dummies indicate thetth year relative to the year of EJR coverage initiation, and the last dummy captures all the years that start from the second year of EJR coverage and onward. The results plotted inFigure 1show evidence against reverse causality: the coefficients for the year dummies before EJR coverage are insignificant and show no obvious trend, suggesting that EJR-rated firms do not have a higher frequency of bad news forecasts than their control firms before EJR’s coverage. Furthermore, the positive effect of EJR coverage remains strong two years after its rating initiation. Thus, the dynamic results contradict the view that temporary deterioration in corporate fundamentals drives both investors’ demand for EJR ratings and management bad news guidance. Another form of reverse causality concern is that investors’ expectation about future downside risks prompt them to seek EJR ratings, which may be combined with their existing information set to generate more precise valuation estimates. In other words, the uncertainty over the arrival of bad news complicates the interpretation of the positive relationship between EJR coverage and management disclosure. To alleviate this more nuanced reverse causality concern, I include three additional variables that potentially capture the existence of upcoming bad news. The first variable is lagged management bad news forecasts, which are an important source of forward-looking accounting information for investors (Beyer et al. 2010). Further, managers appear to maintain similar guidance policies over time (Chapman and Green 2018). Thus, it is possible that past management guidance affects investors’ demand for EJR coverage and current guidance simultaneously. The estimated result in column (1) of panel A fromTable 4shows that, whereas past management bad news forecast frequency has a strong positive correlation with future bad news forecasts, including it as a control has little impact on the coefficient estimate forEJR Cover. Table 4.Tests on Selection Issues Table 4.Tests on Selection Issues Notes.This table reports the regressions that test whether the baseline finding is robust to adding additional control variables. In panel A, the additional controls attempt to capture managerial or investors’ expectations about firm prospects. In column (1),𝐿⁢𝑛⁡(𝑁𝑒𝑔𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑣𝑒𝑀⁢𝐹𝑡−1)L⁢n⁡(NegativeM⁢Ft−1)is management bad news forecasts in yeart− 1. Column (2) includes the aggregated absolute changes in institutional investors’ ownership scaled by annual trading volume. Column (3) controls for investors’ residual short interests inBao et al. (2019)constructed as the level of short interest orthogonalized to factors unrelated to firm-specific information. In panel B, the regressions attempt to account for factors that can drive EJR’s coverage and management disclosure at the same time. Column (1) augments the baseline model with theAmihud (2002)illiquidity measure (ILLIQ). Column (2) includes# News Articles, the number of news articles covering a firm from RavenPack. Column (3) controls for whether a firm has debt issues rated by Moody’s. Other control variables are included in the regressions but unreported in the table. The fixed effects included in the regressions are denoted at the bottom of the panels. Standard errors in parentheses are clustered at the firm level. *, **, and *** denote significance level at 10%, 5%, and 1%, respectively. The second measure attempts to capture investor expectations about corporate performance revealed through their trading activities. Studies document that institutional investors’ trading is influenced by their private information (Piotroski and Roulstone 2004) and can generate long-term profits by anticipating earnings surprises (Campbell et al. 2009). Thus, institutional investors’ trading activity conveys their expectation about corporate performance, which may affect their demand for both EJR’s ratings and corporate disclosure. I measure investors’ trading activities by aggregating changes in shareholding across all institutional investors in a given year and then scale this aggregated amount by annual stock trading volume. InTable 4, the result in column (2) of panel A suggests that increases in investors’ trading intensity do predict increases in the frequency of bad news forecasts, consistent with findings in the literature. Importantly, the coefficient estimate forEJR Coverremains unchanged after controlling for investors’ trading intensity. This evidence suggests that the decision of the subgroup of institutional investors to request EJR’s ratings is probably unrelated to aggregate investor expectations. Instead, subscribing investors’ demand for EJR ratings is likely to be driven by idiosyncratic reasons, for example, a lack of in-house research ability (Bhattacharya et al. 2019). The last measure of the existence of bad news is the residual short interest, constructed byBao et al. (2019)to reflect undisclosed bad news that managers may possess. Because short interest reflects negative private information shared by managers and short sellers (e.g.,Khan and Lu 2013),Bao et al. (2019)infer the existence of managers’ private bad news by constructing a residual short interest (Residual SI) measure purged of other noninformation factors.24Building on their insights, I useResidual SIto control for the existence of bad news that may confound the relationship between investors’ demand for EJR’s ratings and management disclosure. If investors purchase EJR’s ratings to corroborate their expectations about future bad news, I expect the inclusion ofResidual SIto weaken the relationship betweenEJR Coverand management bad news forecasts. The result in column (3) of panel A provides no support for this alternative interpretation.25Overall, by verifying the parallel trend assumption and controlling for managers’ and investors’ expectations about future downside risks, these analyses alleviate the concern that the causal relationship runs from the arrival of bad news to EJR’s rating coverage. InTable A.2, I directly check whether EJR coverage is related to changes in concurrent corporate events and performance. In terms of firm activities, I observe no changes in corporate acquisition activities, divestiture, or investments (capital expenditure and R&D). In terms of performance, EJR’s coverage does not predict future firm sales, return on equity, or stock return. Overall, I find no changes in major corporate activities around EJR’s coverage initiations. A more nuanced reverse causality concern is that EJR anticipates future improvements in corporate information disclosure and initiates rating coverage on these firms. It is useful to note that EJR initiates rating coverage at investors’ requests. Thus, if EJR’s rating coverage anticipates improvements in firm transparency, this anticipatory effect should come from investors’ information. However, the preceding analyses suggest that, after controlling for several proxies of investor expectations, the relationship between EJR coverage and management bad news guidance remains unchanged. Further, there seem to be compelling reasons to believe that institutional investors will spend more resources to acquire information when they anticipate less firm disclosure because more disclosure can reduce the benefit of costly information acquisition (e.g.,Goldstein and Yang 2019,Pinto 2023). 3.2.2. Omitted Variable Concern. The relationship between EJR coverage and management bad news disclosure may be driven by factors not included in the baseline controls. For example, even if EJR’s ratings help investors follow and monitor management disclosure, EJR’s ratings might be accompanied by other monitoring forces that lead to an overestimation of EJR’s impact. To alleviate this omitted variable concern, I describe issues that I can envisage and construct variables to include them in the regressions. First, the literature suggests that high stock liquidity can encourage investors to acquire more private information by increasing the profitability of informed trading (e.g.,Subrahmanyam and Titman 2001). Stock liquidity can also shape managers’ disclosure incentive by affecting how investors respond to management disclosure (Hope and Liu 2023). If higher stock liquidity incentivizes institutional investors to become more informed through various sources (including EJR’s credit ratings), stock liquidity could drive EJR’s coverage and management disclosure simultaneously. To alleviate this concern, I explicitly control for firms’ stock liquidity by including the illiquidity measure ofAmihud (2002)in the regression. In panel B ofTable 4, the coefficient ofEJR Coverin column (1) remains significant and positive in this test, which suggests that the effect of EJR coverage is not driven by an increase in stock liquidity. Second, it is still possible that monitoring from other market participants is confounding EJR’s rating coverage. For example, if certain events trigger investors’ demand for EJR’s ratings, these events may also attract media coverage, which serves as an external monitor of firm managers (e.g.,Miller 2006). Thus, media coverage could be an important omitted factor that results in a coincidence between EJR coverage and management disclosure. To this end, I compute the annual number of news articles covering a firm since 2001 based on the RavenPack database.26The result in column (2) shows that, despite a reduction in sample size because of the coverage of RavenPack, the association between EJR coverage and bad news disclosure remains after controlling for the number of news articles covering a firm. This result suggests that the effect of EJR does not stem from EJR free riding on other sources of information. Third, I investigate whether the increase in management bad news disclosure is unique to EJR’s coverage or results from additional rating coverage from multiple CRAs.Kronlund (2020)finds that, when firms are rated by a single CRA, their ratings are more likely to be optimistically biased, suggesting the presence of rating shopping.Bonsall et al. (2017)document that issuer-paid CRAs gain an informational advantage over EJR when rating opaque firms because of their better access to corporate management. Thus, it is likely that the coverage of multiple CRAs can better inform investors about issuer fundamentals and discipline management guidance regardless of whether the additional coverage is from investor- or issuer-paid CRAs. To verify whether the main finding picks up this additional rating effect, I retrieve the rating coverage data of Moody’s and include it in the main regression. The result in column (3) shows that Moody’s debt coverage is not associated with changes in management bad news forecasts. The positive and significant coefficient forEJR Coversuggests that the baseline finding is unique to EJR’s coverage, consistent with its monitoring effect arising from the investor-paid compensation model.27 Finally, I adopt the econometric approach inOster (2019)to directly evaluate the impact of unobservables on the relationship between EJR coverage and management bad news disclosure. Oster starts from the insight that, if unobservables are confounding the relationship between an outcome variable and the key explanatory variable, a hypothetical regression with both observable and unobservable variables should yield a weaker coefficient estimate for the main effect (βmin) and a higherR2(Rmax) than the baseline regression (Altonji et al. 2005). Thus, based on the proportional selection relationship (i.e., the selection on observable and unobservable is proportional),28we can specify values forβminandRmaxand bound the relative importance of unobservables to observables or theδstatistic. This approach is widely adopted in applied economic research, including accounting and financial studies (e.g.,Bhagwat et al. 2016,Call et al. 2018,Cohen et al. 2020,Gallemore 2023). Following Oster’s suggestion, I specify𝛽𝑚𝑖𝑛=0βmin=0, a conservative restriction indicating that the hypothetical regression with unobservables would push the main effect to zero. In addition, Oster suggests that, because of measurement errors, even the hypothetical regression with a full set of controls would fail to fully explain outcome variables in many settings. Thus, I follow her recommendation to setRmaxto be 1.3 times theR2from a baseline regression with observable controls. Using the specified parameters and the differences in coefficient andR2between regressions with and without observable controls, I compute theδstatistic to quantify the relative importance between unobservables and observables. Aδgreater than 1.00 suggests a robust main effect because it indicates that, for unobservables to fully explain away the main effect, they need to be as important as the observable controls. In contrast, if𝛿<0δ<0, it suggests that the estimated effect is biased downward. InTable 5, I report estimatedδto evaluate the importance of unobservables in explaining the relationship betweenEJR CoverandNegative MF. In column (1), the regression includes controls in the baseline regression, that is, firm characteristics with firm–cohort and year–cohort fixed effects. In column (2), year–cohort fixed effects are replaced by industry–year–cohort effects. In column (3), I add the six additional controls inTable 4to the baseline regression. Across all three columns,δis estimated to be greater than 1.00. For instance, in column (1),δis estimated to be 2.11, suggesting that unobservables need to be 2.11 times as important as the baseline controls inEquation (2)to render a null effect. This is unlikely given that the baseline controls already include a long list of important time-varying firm variables and all time-invariant firm characteristics. Overall, the evidence based on Oster’s approach further ensures that the impact of EJR coverage on management forecast is unlikely to be driven by omitted variables. Table 5.Evaluating Omitted Variable Bias UsingOster’s (2019)Approach Table 5.Evaluating Omitted Variable Bias UsingOster’s (2019)Approach Notes.This table reports the results of tests that evaluate the robustness of the relationship between EJR coverage and management bad news guidance to unobservable omitted variables. As shown byOster (2019), there is a unique ratio of selection on unobservable variables to selection on controlled variables (i.e., theδstatistic) that matches a zero main effect. I compute theδstatistic by assuming that the inclusion of unobservables would produce a zero main effect and a maximumR2of 1.3 times the regression without unobservables.Modelindicates the regression used to computeδ. For instance, in the bottom row, the model includes all the controls in the baseline regression and the additional controls introduced inTable 4.β without controls(R2without controls) is the coefficient estimate forEJR Cover(R2) when onlyEJR coveris included as the explanatory variable.β with controls(R2with controls) is the coefficient estimate (R2) under the regression specified in the first column.Rmaxequals 1.3 timesR2with controls.δis the ratio of selection on unobserved variables to selection on observed variables that matches a zero main effect andRmax. If𝛿>1δ>1, it indicates that the unobservables need to be at least as important as the included controls to push the effect of EJR coverage to zero. Thus, aδgreater than one is regarded as evidence that the omitted variable bias is unlikely to explain away the main effect. To summarize, although endogeneity is a perennial issue that no empirical test can entirely rule out, I conduct a battery of tests to alleviate endogeneity concerns and find consistent results. Whereas each test has its limitations, the balance of the evidence points to a causal relationship that runs from EJR’s rating coverage to management bad news forecasts.
article.4. Additional Tests: In this section, I probe into the specific mechanisms through which EJR’s coverage exerts an impact on management bad news disclosure by studying cross-sectional variation in the coverage effect. Next, I adopt the return-based approach inKothari et al. (2009)to detect the impact of EJR coverage on managerial bad news hoarding. Third, I examine changes in earnings management after EJR’s coverage to corroborate the results of the main analysis on bad news disclosure through management guidance. Finally, I assess the robustness of the main finding to alternative model specifications. 4.1. Cross-Sectional Analyses I examine whether the effect of EJR’s coverage exhibits cross-sectional variation that is consistent with the main hypothesis. Specifically, the hypothesis predicts that the baseline finding will be stronger when EJR’s ratings inform investors to a greater extent, which leads to possible large changes in investors’ perceptions about managers’ private information and nondisclosure. In addition, the finding will be stronger when managers have a stronger ex ante incentive to withhold bad news. I find evidence consistent with the two predictions. First, I expect the amount of potential information conveyed through EJR’s ratings to be larger when its ratings contradict those of S&P. Under such a scenario, it is likely that EJR initiates rating coverage with a different perspective about the issuers’ rating quality, especially when EJR’s ratings are less favorable and, thus, signal inflated S&P ratings. I, thus, group EJR-rated firms according to whether their EJR ratings are less optimistic than S&P’s rating. To avoid a mechanical correlation between unfavorable EJR ratings and corporate bad news arrival, I compare EJR’s ratings with S&P’s ratings at the year of EJR’s rating initiation. The matched sample is then divided into two subgroups: one includes EJR-rated firms with a less favorable EJR rating than S&P rating and their matched control firms, and the other includes the remaining matched firm pairs. I estimate the baseline specification with the two subsamples and report the results in panel A ofTable 6. The estimates in columns (1) and (2) show that EJR’s coverage effect on disclosure is driven mainly by firms with less favorable first time EJR ratings, confirming that the ability of EJR’s ratings to inform investors (especially about potential downside risks) pushes managers to disclose more. Table 6.Cross-Sectional Analyses Table 6.Cross-Sectional Analyses Notes.Panel A presents the regression results regarding how EJR’s coverage effect on bad news forecasts varies with the informativeness of EJR’s ratings relative to S&P’s ratings. The dependent variable is the natural log of one plus the number of bad news management forecasts. In column (1), the sample includes firms with less favorable EJR ratings than S&P’s ratings at the event year and their matched control firms; the sample in column (2) includes the rest matched firm pairs. In column (3), the sample includes firms with S&P ratings around the investment grade–high yield boundary at the event year and their matched control firms; the sample in column (4) includes the rest firm pairs. Panel B reports the results regarding how the EJR coverage effect depends on corporate managers’ incentive to withhold bad news. In column (1), the sample includes EJR-rated firms with above-median equity delta at the event year and their matched control firms; the sample in column (2) includes the rest firm pairs. In column (3), the sample includes EJR-rated firms with below-median litigation risk measured at the event year and their matched control firms; the sample in column (4) includes the rest firm pairs.Differencerefers to the differences in coefficient estimates forEJR Coverbetween subsample pairs;P-value is the corresponding one-tailedp-value from tests of coefficient differences. The fixed effects included in the regressions are denoted at the bottom of the panels. Standard errors clustered at the firm level are reported in parentheses. *, **, and *** denote significance level at 10%, 5%, and 1%, respectively. Second, I use an indirect approach to measure the likelihood that S&P’s ratings are inflated relative to EJR’s ratings. Specifically, I identify firms with S&P ratings hovering around the investment grade–high-yield boundary. Because of the discontinuous increase in financing costs when the firms’ ratings slide across the investment grade–high-yield boundary, firms rated near the boundary have a stronger incentive for rating shopping or pressing for better ratings from issuer-paid CRAs. To identify such firms, I construct a dummy variable (IG–HY) that equals one if their S&P rating is within one notch below or above the Better Business Bureau (BBB) rating when EJR initiates rating coverage for these firms and zero otherwise. I group cohorts by whether the EJR-rated firm in a matched firm pair is an IG–HY firm and separately reestimateEquation (2)for the two groups of cohorts. The regression results in columns (3) and (4) from panel A ofTable 6suggest that the impact of EJR coverage is stronger when S&P’s ratings are more susceptible to conflict of interest. Thus, the results in panel A ofTable 6substantiate the hypothesis that it is EJR’s ability to inform investors and challenge existing information sources that really matters. In contrast, if some omitted factors (such as investors’ attention or concerns) are driving investors to solicit EJR ratings and managers to disclose more at the same time, we do not expect the baseline result to depend on whether EJR’s ratings are more or less informative than S&P’s ratings. Third, I predict a larger EJR coverage effect when managers have a stronger ex ante incentive to withhold bad news and construct two managerial incentives for bad news hoarding. The first measure is the likelihood of shareholder litigation faced by managers. High litigation concerns constrain managers’ propensity to withhold bad news by shaping the cost–benefit trade-off in doing so (e.g.,Skinner 1997,Houston et al. 2019). The second measure relates to managers’ incentive to withhold bad news for their wealth tied to their firms’ stock price performance.Kothari et al. (2009)show that managers appear to withhold more bad news when they hold more stocks in their firms. Thus, the marginal benefit of EJR’s disciplining effect on bad news hoarding will become weaker when managers already withhold less news in the absence of EJR’s coverage. To measure the litigation risk an individual firm faces, I follow previous studies to use the empirical model of litigation likelihood estimated byKim and Skinner (2012).29To measure managers’ stakes in their company, I followCore and Guay (2002)andColes et al. (2006)to compute the equity delta, which quantifies how the value of managers’ equity and option compensation responds to a 1% increase in share price. I split the matched sample by whether the EJR-rated firm in each matched firm pair has below- or above-median estimated litigation probability (or equity delta). Consistent with the expectation, the regression results tabulated in panel B ofTable 6show stronger results when managers have a stronger incentive to withhold bad news. In addition, these findings based on bad news holding incentives appear to refute the anticipatory effect of EJR coverage (i.e., EJR’s coverage anticipates improvement in firm disclosure quality), which implies stronger main results when rated firms’ managers are less likely to withhold bad news (or more likely to commit to greater firm transparency). 4.2. Return-Based Evidence on Bad News Disclosure The baseline analyses rely on observable managerial earnings guidance to infer their propensity to disclose bad news. An alternative approach in the literature is to examine the extent to which stock prices anticipate future corporate events.Kothari et al. (2009)document a stronger stock price reaction to bad news than good news and attribute this finding to managers’ incentives to conceal bad news until subsequent public announcements. An advantage of this return-based approach is that the test is conditional on the existence of firm-specific news that will be revealed in upcoming disclosure events, eschewing the empirical challenge arising from the uncertainty over the existence of private bad news possessed by managers. FollowingKothari et al. (2009), I examine stock price behavior surrounding management earnings revisions. If EJR’s rating coverage pushes corporate managers to increase the timeliness of bad news disclosure, I expect the gap between the market reaction to bad and good earnings news to narrow after its coverage. I test this prediction by setting up a forecast-level regression model: Table 7reports the regression results ofEquation (3). The sample includes unbundled earnings forecasts. In column (1), the coefficient of𝑁𝑒𝑔𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑣𝑒𝑀⁢𝐹𝑖,𝑞×𝐸𝐽𝑅𝐶𝑜𝑣𝑒𝑟𝑖,𝑞NegativeM⁢Fi,q×EJRCoveri,qis significantly positive, suggesting a reduction in stock price reaction to bad news earnings revision when a firm is rated by EJR prior to its earnings guidance. In contrast, the coefficient on𝑁𝑒𝑔𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑣𝑒𝑀⁢𝐹𝑖,𝑞×𝑆&𝑃𝐶𝑜𝑣𝑒𝑟𝑖,𝑞NegativeM⁢Fi,q×S&PCoveri,qis insignificant. Thus, the results provide additional support to the main hypothesis that EJR’s coverage curbs managerial bad news hoarding, thus reducing the stock market’s reaction to negative earnings news. In addition, consistent with the results fromTable 3, coverage by S&P is not associated with meaningful changes in the timeliness of stock prices’ ability to reflect negative earnings news. Table 7.Return-Based Evidence on Bad News Disclosure Table 7.Return-Based Evidence on Bad News Disclosure Notes.This table presents regressions that test potential changes in stock price reactions to management bad news disclosure associated with EJR coverage. The observations include unbundled management earnings guidance. In column (1), the dependent variable is the size-adjusted abnormal return over the trading days [−1, 1] relative to the date of management bad news forecasts. In column (2), the dependent variable is the size-adjusted abnormal return over the trading days [−90, −2] relative to the management forecast date.EJR Cover(S&P Cover) is a dummy variable that equals one for a firm–quarter if the firm is rated by EJR (S&P) over the days [−90, −1] relative to the earnings announcement date and zero otherwise.Negative MFindicates management earnings forecasts that fall below contemporaneous analyst consensus.MF Newsis the difference between management forecasted value and analyst consensus value scaled by stock price at the end of the previous quarter. Control variables include loss indicator, fourth fiscal quarter indicator, firm size, book-to-market ratio, number of analysts, institutional ownership, stock price volatility, leverage, beta, earnings persistence, and earnings volatility. The regressions control for firm–cohort and year–cohort fixed effects. Standard errors are clustered by firm and reported in parentheses. *, **, and *** denote significance level at 10%, 5%, and 1%, respectively. An alternative interpretation of these results is that firms’ earnings quality deteriorates at the time of EJR’s rating coverage, especially for negative earnings. As a result, investors become less responsive to earnings announcements with lower credibility. To further assess this interpretation’s validity, I examine the abnormal returns within the quarter running up to a management forecast date. If EJR’s coverage is accompanied by lower earnings quality, the preforecast stock return should also have a weaker correlation with the upcoming unexpected earnings, that is, the coefficient estimates of𝑁𝑒𝑔𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑣𝑒𝑀⁢𝐹𝑖,𝑞×𝐸𝐽𝑅𝐶𝑜𝑣𝑒𝑟𝑖,𝑞NegativeM⁢Fi,q×EJRCoveri,qshould be negative. Results in column (2) provide no supportive evidence, suggesting that any difference in the quality of negative earnings news between EJR-rated firms and the control firms is unlikely to drive the results inTable 7. The overall findings, thus, support the interpretation that EJR’s rating coverage weakens managerial incentives to withhold bad news. 4.3. Analysis of Earnings Quality In the main analyses, I focus on management bad news disclosure in the form of earnings guidance. However, in addition to earnings guidance, managers can influence the dissemination of bad news through other disclosure channels; for instance, they can manipulate the quality of reported financial earnings. Indeed, studies document that firm managers influence CRAs’ perceptions of credit risk through earnings management (Alissa et al. 2013). Upward earnings management can help firms avoid rating downgrades (Liu et al. 2018). One potential reason for earnings management influencing credit ratings is that issuer-paid CRAs are more likely to condone earnings management activities because of the conflict of interest from the issuer pay model.31To the extent that EJR’s ratings can better inform investors and are less susceptible to rated firms’ opportunistic accounting manipulations, its coverage may help reduce the effectiveness of upward earnings management in influencing ratings (and, thus, investors’ perceptions). Moreover, EJR’s coverage may deter other issuer-paid CRAs from condoning earnings management by increasing their reputational costs (Xia 2014). Thus, I expect a reduction in rated firms’ discretionary accruals after EJR’s coverage. To test this prediction, I measure firms’ upward earnings management intensity as the signed residuals estimated from several models of discretionary accruals: the modifiedJones (1991)model, theDechow and Dichev (2002)model, and the performance-matching approach advocated byKothari et al. (2005). These models are estimated with annual firm data for each SIC two-digit industry–year combination with at least 20 observations.32The residuals from each of these models are used as dependent variables and regressed against the set of control variables inEquation (2). Table 8reports the results. In column (1), I observe a 0.9 percentage point reduction in the discretionary accrual estimated by the modified Jones model. The reduction is statistically significant at the conventional level and also economically meaningful as it is around 12.5% of the standard deviation of discretionary accrual in the sample. In columns (2) and (3), similar results are documented when discretionary accrual is measured using the performance-matching approach inKothari et al. (2005)or theDechow and Dichev (2002)model. Consistent with findings from analyses of management bad news forecasts, the coverage of S&P is not associated with changes in discretionary accruals. Because upward earnings management activities help managers portray an overly positive image of their firms’ operations, the results for the reduction in discretionary accrual following EJR’s coverage provide the consistent finding that EJR’s rating coverage deters managers from withholding unfavorable news. Table 8.EJR’s Coverage and Corporate Earnings Management Table 8.EJR’s Coverage and Corporate Earnings Management Notes.This table reports the regressions that examine how EJR coverage affects rated firms’ earnings management activities. The sample consists of firms rated by EJR and matched control firms in the 10-year window around EJR’s coverage. Financial firms are excluded. In columns (1)–(3), the dependent variables are the signed discretionary accrual estimated from the modifiedJones (1991)model, the performance-adjusted accrual inKothari et al. (2005), and theDechow and Dichev (2002)model, respectively.EJR Coveris a dummy variable that equals one if a firm is rated by EJR during the year and zero otherwise.S&P Coveris an indicator that equals one if S&P rates a firm in a year. The definitions of other control variables are provided inTable A.1. The fixed effects included in each regression are denoted at the bottom of the table. Standard errors in parentheses are clustered at the firm level. *, **, and *** denote significance level at 10%, 5%, and 1%, respectively. 4.4. Robustness Checks First, I design falsification tests to mitigate the concern that the effect of EJR coverage is driven by spurious trends. Specifically, EJR’s rating coverage initiations exhibit a clustered pattern (Xia 2014). The concentration of EJR’s rating initiation around certain years may lead to spurious results. To evaluate whether the documented relationship betweenEJR Coverand management bad news forecasts is driven by spurious trends, I randomly assign EJR coverage status each year, keeping the number of firms receiving pseudo-coverage equal to the number of actual coverage initiations by EJR. I then reestimate the baseline regression with the pseudo-initiation sample and compare the estimated coefficient onEJR Coverwith the actual coefficient in column (2) ofTable 3. This procedure is performed 1,000 times. Results plotted inFigure 2show that the mean, standard deviation, and maximum coefficient estimated from the falsification tests are 0.001, 0.022, and 0.091, respectively. The actual coefficient (0.099) is around four times the standard deviation above the mean and larger than the maximum value from the distribution. Thus, the falsification tests suggest that assigning EJR coverage to the right firms is essential, and the results are not merely spurious correlations because of event clustering. Second, I perform a number of additional analyses to assess whether the main results are robust to alternative sample selection, outcome variables, matching procedures, and model specifications. For brevity, I tabulate only the coefficient estimates forEJR CoverinTable A.3. I verify that the baseline results are robust to (a) excluding EJR coverage initiations accompanied by potential information events to alleviate the concern of confounding corporate news,33(b) finding one matched control firm with the closest propensity score for each EJR-rated firm, (c) removing the restriction that the propensity score between EJR-rated firms and their control firms should be less than 1%, (d) focusing on a shorter window around the event years, (e) excluding firm–years without management forecasts, (f) using a fixed effects Poisson model to avoid the potential bias arising from using log-transformed values as the outcome variable (Cohn et al. 2022), (g) using the untransformed count of management bad news forecasts as the outcome variable in the stacked DiD regression, (h) running the traditional two-way fixed effects regression (firm and year fixed effects) with the matched sample.
article.5. Conclusion: CRAs play an influential role in the financial market, and the information content of credit ratings has long been the subject of extensive academic research. However, after the poor performance of CRAs during dramatic market events such as Enron’s collapse and the 2008 financial crisis, the efficacy of CRAs’ fundamental role as information intermediaries has been widely questioned. CRAs’ poor performance also spurred research into the performance of an alternative type of CRA, that is, investor-paid CRAs that are compensated by investors. The general conclusion from the literature is that investor-paid CRAs outperform their issuer-paid counterparts in terms of rating accuracy and timeliness. Notably, the current view on CRAs’ informational role focuses on the extent to which they help to incorporate information into security prices and supposes that CRAs have no impact on the behavior of rated firms. This paper presents the view that CRAs’ role in promoting transparency is more than just as an initial information provider. Specifically, CRAs’ incentive to incorporate information into their ratings has a disciplining effect on firm managers, who can disseminate or produce information in the first place. Providing support for this disciplining mechanism, this paper shows that the coverage of EJR, an investor-paid CRA, leads to a higher frequency of bad news disclosure in the form of management earnings guidance and a reduction in upward earnings management. The coverage of S&P, a representative issuer-paid CRA, in contrast, has no meaningful impact on corporate disclosure. Further evidence suggests that EJR’s impact on disclosure comes from its ability to inform investors and deter managerial bad news hoarding. The results survive an extensive list of endogeneity tests and robustness checks. The findings in this paper paint a more complete picture of CRAs’ informational role. That is, investor-paid CRAs can play a meaningful monitoring role in regard to rated firms’ disclosure. This monitoring role may be particularly useful as a preemptive force to mitigate the accumulation of information asymmetry and asset overvaluation in financial markets, which is the main feature of several financial failures. Given that the credit rating industry is still dominated by three issuer-paid CRAs, the paper also generates policy implications that investor-paid ratings should play more than just a marginal role in the rating industry.
